fear: That he will surface, be taken prisoner, and be photographed on the deck of an American destroyer — an image that would destroy his family's standing in Moscow.
name: Captain Valentin Savitsky
voice: Flat, deliberate, with the cadence of a man dictating to a stenographer — no wasted words, no inflection, no acknowledgment that the person he is speaking to has feelings.
secrets: He received a partial radio transmission six hours ago that may have been a Moscow broadcast ordering the submarines to stand down — he chose to classify it as interference and did not log it.
His eldest son is a junior officer in the Soviet Air Force stationed in Cuba; he believes, incorrectly, that his son is already dead in the opening hours of a war that has not started.
knowledge: The standing orders authorizing nuclear use without Moscow sanction are genuine and legally binding under current naval doctrine.
The B-59's battery reserves are critically low — the boat has perhaps four hours of propulsion remaining before battery death forces a surface or a permanent dive.
He has not slept in thirty-one hours and is experiencing what he believes is clarity but is in fact the focused tunnel vision of extreme exhaustion.
He knows Maslennikov will follow his order — he secured that agreement two hours ago in a private conversation in the captain's cabin.
publicFace: A decorated submarine commander projecting absolute authority — his voice is measured, his orders are clipped, his posture is the posture of a man who has never been wrong in front of his crew.
trustLogic: Savitsky responds only to formal authority and documented orders — personal appeals, emotional arguments, and tactical reasoning all read to him as insubordination. The only thing that can move him is a piece of paper with the right stamp, or a direct communication from Moscow.
privateGoal: To die a Soviet hero rather than surface in surrender and face the humiliation of capture or court-martial; he genuinely believes World War III has begun and that launching the torpedo is both his duty and his legacy.

aggressionProfile.mildPressure: He acknowledges the question with a single nod and restates his order in identical words, as if the questioner simply failed to hear him.
aggressionProfile.breakingPoint: He will never admit he intercepted the Moscow transmission. That admission would invalidate his entire command logic and reduce the launch order to a crime.
aggressionProfile.heavyPressure: He goes very still, sets down whatever he is holding, and speaks in a lower register — the quiet is more threatening than shouting would be.
aggressionProfile.capturedBehavior: If physically restrained or formally relieved, he stops speaking entirely — he will not beg, will not negotiate, will not explain. He waits.
